summary: Jeff Atwood argues that digital software distribution in 2007 is fundamentally broken because consumers pay the same or higher prices for downloads compared to physical retail copies, despite the dramatically lower distribution costs for vendors. He demonstrates this with examples from Steam, Microsoft Vista, and EA, showing how digital purchases cost more while eliminating manufacturing and shipping costs entirely. The post criticizes vendors for exploiting digital distribution to maximize profits rather than passing savings to consumers, and notes the additional burden of DRM and proprietary download software. Atwood concludes that commercial software should learn from open source's download-by-design approach before market forces make physical distribution obsolete.
key_points: Digital downloads often cost the same or more than physical retail copies, despite eliminating manufacturing, shipping, and retail middleman costs
Direct sales allow vendors to keep 90% of revenue versus roughly 10% through retail channels, yet prices remain identical
Digital distribution adds friction for consumers through DRM wrappers, proprietary downloaders, and lengthy download times that physical media doesn't require
Vendors are reluctant to undercut their retail distribution partners like Best Buy, even though the economics clearly favor digital
The current model only appeals to 'the rich and impatient' while average consumers rationally choose cheaper retail options
money_quotes: Even when the download option is available, you end up paying the same price as retail or even more.
Paying a premium for the privilege of downloading software is complete ripoff, and yet it happens all the time.
You keep 90% of the revenues, and that's gravy.
Does that really make up for the fact that you just increased their profit margin on the sale by a factor of five?
Digital software distribution should cost less.
The digital distribution of commercial software is intentionally crippled.
It's only useful for the rich and impatient, a fact vendors exploit to line their pockets with obscene profit margins.
With every download at retail prices, you're effectively paying vendors five times as much for the same software, and that's a huge ripoff.
key_insight: Digital software distribution's promise of efficiency and lower costs has been deliberately subverted by vendors who maintain retail pricing while pocketing the savings from eliminated manufacturing and distribution costs.
